title: Derived categories of cyclic covers and their branch divisors
speaker: Alexander Perry
abstract: Given a variety $Y$ with a rectangular Lefschetz decomposition of its derived category, I will discuss an interesting relation between the derived categories of a cyclic cover of $Y$ and its branch divisor. As examples, I will describe the cases of cyclic cubic fourfolds and "special Gushel--Mukai varieties," where we get a description of the equivariant category of certain K3 categories. This is joint work with Alexander Kuznetsov.

proof_stages.label: Rectangular Lefschetz semiorthogonal framework
proof_stages.summary: The talk fixes notation for derived categories, recalls semiorthogonal decompositions, and isolates the standing hypothesis that D(Y) has a rectangular Lefschetz decomposition with repeated block B twisted by O(1).

proof_stages.label: Residual categories for the branch divisor and cyclic cover
proof_stages.summary: Given a smooth divisor Z in |O_Y(nd)| and the degree-n cyclic cover X -> Y branched over Z, restriction and pullback from Y are shown to embed the Lefschetz block B under numerical bounds, producing semiorthogonal decompositions with residual pieces A_Z and A_X.

proof_stages.label: Equivariant main theorem
proof_stages.summary: The deck group mu_n acts on X and preserves A_X, and the main theorem states that n-1 fully faithful functors Phi_k from A_Z to the equivariant category A_X^{mu_n} form a semiorthogonal decomposition, with a sharper reconstruction statement when n=2.

proof_stages.label: First decomposition of the equivariant category
proof_stages.summary: The proof begins by equivariantizing the decomposition of D(X), using Elagin's theorem and the trivial action on pulled-back B-blocks to write D(X)^{mu_n} as A_X^{mu_n} together with a counted collection of B-blocks indexed by characters.

proof_stages.label: Root-stack decomposition of the same category
proof_stages.summary: A second semiorthogonal decomposition of D(X)^{mu_n} is obtained from fully faithful functors f_k^* and j_{k*}, giving one copy of D(Y) and n-1 copies of D(Z), which after inserting the earlier decompositions yields n-1 copies of A_Z plus the same number of ambient B-blocks.

proof_stages.label: Mutation comparison and construction of Phi_k
proof_stages.summary: A sequence of mutation functors rearranges the second decomposition so that its B-blocks coincide with those in the first decomposition, forcing the remaining orthogonal part to be the desired semiorthogonal sequence of images of A_Z and giving explicit formulas for Phi_k.

proof_stages.label: Character-action reconstruction for n=2
proof_stages.summary: Finally, the character group action on A_X^{mu_n} is recalled to recover A_X by equivariantization, and in the double-cover case this action is transported to A_Z and made explicit via rotation functors.

examples.name: Beilinson collection on projective space
examples.note: The collection of line bundles on projective space makes the abstract definition of a semiorthogonal decomposition and exceptional object concrete, without contributing to the proof of the cyclic-cover theorem.
examples.engagement_reason: The Beilinson decomposition of projective space is written down and explained as a standard example, but no property is checked or computed on that collection itself.

passes.reason: The Beilinson collection is a standard highly regular collection on the maximally homogeneous projective space, not a sporadic exception or counterexample.

examples.name: Gushel-Mukai double-cover and branch examples
examples.note: A hyperplane section of Gr(2,5), its quadratic section, and the associated double cover illustrate the theorem by producing related residual categories for Gushel-Mukai varieties in different dimensions.
examples.engagement_reason: The Gushel-Mukai double-cover and branch case is set up explicitly, its derived-category decompositions are written, the theorem is applied, and invariants and Serre functors are analyzed.

passes.reason: The Gushel–Mukai double-cover/branch case is used to show that the Enriques/K3-like categorical analogy need not be realized by actual smooth-projective derived categories.

examples.name: Cyclic cubic fourfold branched over a cubic threefold
examples.note: Taking Y=P^4, Z a cubic threefold, and X the cyclic cubic fourfold gives a concrete n=3 case where the equivariant K3 category of X decomposes into two copies of the residual category of Z.
examples.engagement_reason: The cyclic cubic fourfold is given by an explicit triple-cover equation, the decompositions of the cubic threefold and fourfold are computed from the lemma, and the theorem is applied.

passes.reason: The cyclic cubic fourfold is a systematic cyclic-cover example illustrating the n=3 theorem rather than refuting a claim.

examples.name: Double cyclic cubic fourfold
examples.note: When the cubic threefold branch divisor is itself cyclic, iterating the theorem yields a mu_3 x mu_3 action whose equivariant category has a full exceptional decomposition, illustrating the strength of the result in a special rational cubic-fourfold case.
examples.engagement_reason: The double cyclic cubic fourfold is treated with explicit coordinates, induced group actions, decompositions with counts of exceptional objects, and geometric consequences such as rationality and K3 equivalence.

passes.reason: The double cyclic cubic fourfold is a special member of a systematic cyclic family used to exhibit the theorem’s behavior, not a classification exception or counterexample.
the double cyclic cubic fourfold is a special but non-sporadic iterated cyclic cover used constructively to produce a simple equivariant category.
